mission.title: Install and Configure Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS)
mission.description: Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) is a managed Kubernetes service that simplifies the deployment and management of Kubernetes clusters on AWS. It is ideal for users who want to leverage AWS's infrastructure while minimizing operational overhead. Key trade-offs include reliance on AWS services and potential vendor lock-in.

steps.title: Install the AWS CLI
steps.description:
The AWS CLI is required to interact with AWS services, including EKS.

```bash

curl 'https://awscli.amazonaws.com/AWSCLIV2.pkg' -o 'AWSCLIV2.pkg'

sudo installer -pkg AWSCLIV2.pkg -target /

aws --version

```

> Download the AWS CLI package.
> Install the AWS CLI.
> Verify the installation.

steps.title: Create an EKS cluster
steps.description:
This command creates an EKS cluster with the specified Kubernetes version and node count.

```bash

aws eks create-cluster --name my-cluster --kubernetes-version 1.31 --role-arn arn:aws:iam::YOUR_ACCOUNT_ID:role/EKS-Cluster-Role --resources-vpc-config subnetIds=subnet-12345678,subnet-87654321

```

> Replace YOUR_ACCOUNT_ID with your actual AWS account ID.

steps.title: Update kubeconfig
steps.description:
This command updates your kubeconfig file to allow kubectl to interact with your new EKS cluster.

```bash

aws eks update-kubeconfig --name my-cluster

```

> This sets the context for kubectl.

steps.title: Verify the cluster nodes
steps.description:
This command checks the status of the nodes in your EKS cluster.

```bash

kubectl get nodes

```

> You should see the nodes in 'Ready' status.

steps.title: Set up monitoring and autoscaling
steps.description:
This step involves setting up Cluster Autoscaler and monitoring tools for your EKS cluster.

```bash

kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kubernetes/autoscaler/master/cluster-autoscaler/cloudprovider/aws/examples/cluster-autoscaler-example.yaml

kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kubernetes/kube-state-metrics/master/examples/standard/kube-state-metrics-deployment.yaml

```

> This installs the Cluster Autoscaler.
> This installs kube-state-metrics for monitoring.

uninstall.title: Delete the EKS cluster
uninstall.description:
This command deletes the EKS cluster and all associated resources, which may lead to data loss.

```bash

aws eks delete-cluster --name my-cluster

```

> This will remove the cluster and its resources.

uninstall.title: Clean up orphaned resources
uninstall.description:
This command helps to identify and clean up any orphaned resources such as load balancers and PVCs.

```bash

aws ec2 describe-load-balancers --query 'LoadBalancers[?contains(LoadBalancerName, `my-cluster`)]'

```

> List load balancers associated with the cluster.

upgrade.title: Upgrade the EKS cluster
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you have a backup of your cluster resources and configurations.

```bash

kubectl get all --all-namespaces -o yaml > backup.yaml

aws eks update-cluster-version --name my-cluster --kubernetes-version 1.31

kubectl get nodes

```

> Backup all resources.
> Upgrade the cluster to the specified version.
> Verify that the nodes are in 'Ready' status after the upgrade.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Unable to connect to the server: x509: certificate signed by unknown authority'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This usually indicates an issue with the kubeconfig file or cluster authentication.

**Fix:**
Run 'aws eks update-kubeconfig --name my-cluster' to refresh your kubeconfig.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'No resources found in kube-system namespace.'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the necessary components are not deployed in the kube-system namespace.

**Fix:**
Ensure that the EKS cluster is up and running and that the necessary components are installed.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Cluster is not reachable'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This could be due to network issues or misconfigured VPC settings.

**Fix:**
Check your VPC configuration and ensure that the cluster endpoint is accessible.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Insufficient capacity in your availability zone.'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that there are not enough resources available in the specified availability zone.

**Fix:**
Try creating the cluster in a different availability zone or increase the instance type.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for Kubernetes 1.31 features, including improved scheduling and resource management.
versionNotes.deprecations: Deprecated certain legacy APIs that are no longer supported.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from 1.30, ensure to review the deprecation notices and update your manifests accordingly.

resolution.summary: You should see the output of 'kubectl get nodes' showing the nodes in 'Ready' status, indicating that your EKS cluster is operational.

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have the necessary tools and resources before starting the installation.
